title: A Practical Research Workflow from Prototype to Manuscript
summary: A compact example of how to move from an experimental idea to a repeatable implementation and a clearer paper draft.

sections.heading: Start with the failure case
paragraphs: A research prototype becomes easier to evaluate when it starts from a concrete failure case. Instead of asking whether a method is generally better, I prefer to define the condition where the current pipeline breaks, then test whether the new idea removes that limitation.
This keeps experiments focused and makes the eventual manuscript easier to structure.

sections.heading: Build the repeatable path
points: Keep raw data, intermediate results, and final figures in separate directories.
Use scripts for repeated evaluation steps so that figures can be regenerated after code changes.
Write down parameter choices near the experiment rather than only in a final draft.

sections.heading: Draft around evidence
paragraphs: A paper draft is clearer when each section is tied to a specific piece of evidence. The method section explains what was changed, the experiments show where the change matters, and the discussion states what remains unresolved.
